content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 5, 2017

Kristin Pelletier
Acting City Attorney
City of Anaheim
200 S. Anaheim Blvd., Suite 356
Anaheim, CA 92805

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-17-144

Dear Ms. Pelletier:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented – the Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as the finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature and not limited to specific governmental decisions, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Barnes participate in governmental decisions involving Disneyland given that her husband previously worked for (and received income from) the American Broadcast Company Inc. ("ABC"), and both Disneyland and ABC are owned by the Walt Disney Company?

CONCLUSION

No. Councilmember Barnes is prohibited from taking part in decisions with a foreseeable and material effect on Disneyland.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-17-144
Page No. 2

FACTS

Both ABC and Disneyland are majority-owned by the Walt Disney Company, which is a
publicly traded holding company. According to its 2016 Annual Report, the Walt Disney Company
operates in four business segments: Media Networks, Parks and Resorts, Studio Entertainment, and
Consumer Products and Interactive Media. ABC is in the Media Networks segment. Disneyland and
Walt Disney Parks and Resorts, US, Inc. operate in the Parks and Resorts segment. The actual
corporate structure is rather complex. In short:

• ABC is 100% owned by ABC Enterprises Inc.

• ABC Enterprises Inc. is 100% owned by Disney Enterprises Inc.

• Disney Enterprises Inc. is 100% owned by the Walt Disney Company.

• Disneyland is 100% owned by Walt Disney Parks & Resorts, U.S., Inc.

• Walt Disney Parks & Resorts, U.S., Inc. is 99.2% owned by Disney Enterprises Inc.

• Disney Enterprises Inc. is 100% owned by the Walt Disney Company.

Councilmember Barnes's husband, Scott Barnes, previously worked for ABC as a crew
member on an ABC television show. Mr. Barnes left this position on March 16, 2017. The checks
and tax forms received by Mr. Barnes for this work were issued by Disney Worldwide Services,
Inc., which is the payroll processor for the various Disney-owned entities and a subsidiary of
Disney Enterprises Inc.

Considering Disneyland's prominence in the City of Anaheim, Councilmember Barnes asks
whether her interest in her husband's income from ABC disqualifies her from decisions that may
affect Disneyland.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from taking part in governmental decisions in which
they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial interest in
a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any of the official's interests specified in Section 87103.

As pertinent to this request, an official has an interest in any source of income aggregating
$500 or more received by, or promised to, the official within the 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c)) including any community property share in the income of a spouse (Section
82030(a)). From the facts provided, Mr. Barnes was employed by ABC until March 2017 and
received income aggregating to more than $1000. Accordingly, Councilmember Barnes has an
interest in ABC as a source of income that potentially disqualifies her from decisions with a
foreseeable material effect on ABC until approximately March of 2018.

File No. 1-17-144
Page No. 3

The determinative issue for purposes of this letter is whether the Councilmember's interest
in ABC potentially disqualifies her from a decision with a foreseeable material effect on
Disneyland. Former Regulation 18703.1(c) expressly stated that "[a]n official has an economic
interest in a business entity which is a parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business
entity in which the official has one of the interests defined in [Section] 87103(a) or (d)." While this
bright-line rule was removed from the conflict of interest regulations, there is no indication that an
effect on an interest in a parent, subsidiary, or otherwise related business should not be given
consideration for purposes of determining whether an official is disqualified. In fact, language in
current Regulation 18700(c)(6) defining those interests that are potentially disqualifying under the
Act still states "[f]or a "parent, subsidiary or otherwise related business entity, see Regulation
18700.2," which defines when a business is a parent, subsidiary, or related business entity.

As a result, since removing former Regulation 18703.1(c), we have continued to consider
the effect on an interest in a parent, subsidiary, or otherwise related business when determining
disqualification. For example, we determined that an official with an interest in the stock of a large
parent company was not disqualified from regulatory decisions in which a small subsidiary was not
explicitly involved because the decision would not have a foreseeable or material effect on the
parent company. (Gasser Advice Letter, No. 1-16-250.) We have also advised an official that a
foreseeable financial effect on a directly involved interest in the stock of a company was material if
the company "or any of its related entities" initiates the proceeding, or is the subject of an action
taken by the agency, the effect of which is directed solely at the entity. (Chmura Advice Letter, No.
1-17-051.)

Accordingly, without the bright-line rule, we must now determine when an official with an
interest in a business entity is disqualified from a decision with an effect on a parent, subsidiary, or
related business on a case-by-case basis. In making this determination we are guided generally by
principles outlined in the Act. First, as mandated by Section 81001, "[t]he people find and declare
… [p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interest or the financial interest of persons
who have supported them." Second, the Act "should be liberally construed to accomplish its
purposes." (Section 81003.)

In this case, we must recognize that income does not necessarily come from a single isolated
source. For example, commission income received as a broker or agent is attributed to the actual
client as well as the business entity through which the broker or agency conducts the business.
(Regulation 18700.1(c).) Likewise, an official with an interest in a business entity also has an
interest in any individual owning a 50 percent or greater share of the business or with the power to
direct or cause the direction of the management and policy of the business. (Regulation
18700.1(a)(2).)

Councilmember Barnes has a community property interest in her husband's income from
ABC, which is directly paid by Disney Worldwide Services, Inc. Moreover, each identified
subsidiary of the Walt Disney Company is wholly owned or nearly wholly owned by the Walt
Disney Company. Based upon these facts, any bias resulting from the income from ABC extends to
Disneyland as well as the Walt Disney Company and each of the subsidiaries identified.
Accordingly, we find that Councilmember Barnes is disqualified from any decision with a

File No. 1-17-144
Page No. 4

foreseeable and material effect on Disneyland, the Walt Disney Company, or any of the other
identified subsidiaries of the Walt Disney Company.

In light of this conclusion, Councilmember Barnes is disqualified from any decision in
Disneyland is explicitly involved because the effect of a decision on a business explicitly
involved is presumed foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and is material under 18702.1(a). A
business is explicitly involved if the business is a named party in the decision or if the decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of a contract with or entitlement to
the business or the business's license or permit. (Regulation 18701(a).)

If Disneyland is not explicitly involved in the decision, a financial effect is foreseeable if it
"can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical . . . absent
extraordinary circumstances beyond the public official's control." (Regulation 18701(b).) A
financial effect is material if "a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price
of the business entity's publicly traded stock." (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Please note that the determination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts
involved in each decision. We recommend you contact our office if you need additional advice with
respect to a particular decision before the City of Anaheim. Also, please be aware that Section 1090
generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from participating in the
making of contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Please contact us for additional advice if a
potential contract is at issue.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
